ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic lamb chop food product entry; no specific Chinese hand-grabbed preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat of sheep (lamb), fresh or chilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific entry for Chinese-style hand-grabbed lamb chops. The closest parent category for fresh/chilled lamb meat is used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 refers to lamb chop as a substance; no specific Chinese preparation variant exists. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 serving of hand-grabbed lamb chops (approximately 2–3 chops)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of hand-grabbed lamb chops consists of approximately 2 to 3 bone-in chops, weighing around 250 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, loin chop, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 17283)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Chinese-style hand-grabbed lamb chops exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy broiled lamb loin chop values are used as the closest defensible analogue for the cooked lamb component. Seasoning additions (cumin, chilli, sesame) contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical quantities and are noted but not separately itemised. Values represent the cooked lamb chop meat and bone-adjacent fat as consumed.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: A Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — Meat and Protein Foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat and protein foods contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and have an effectively zero glycemic impact. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) confirm that meat products do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response. Per Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is set to low. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving of hand-grabbed lamb chops provides approximately 180 mg sodium from the meat itself; however, restaurant preparations with added salt, soy-based marinades, or seasoning blends can substantially increase sodium load, and individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hand-grabbed lamb chops contain effectively zero digestible carbohydrate and have no measurable glycemic impact, making them a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and in portions that keep total energy intake within targets.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb chops contain approximately 5.8 g saturated fat and 97 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 100–150 g cooked weight, choose leaner cuts where possible, and balance intake with vegetables and whole grains to manage LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hand-Grabbed Lamb Chops (手抓羊排, Shǒu zhuā yáng pái) is a popular Chinese dish originating from the northwest regions of China, particularly associated with Xinjiang Uyghur and Gansu/Lanzhou culinary traditions. The dish consists of bone-in lamb rib or loin chops that are seasoned generously with cumin, dried chilli flakes, salt, garlic, and sesame seeds, then grilled or roasted at high heat until the exterior is charred and aromatic while the interior remains juicy and tender. The name 'hand-grabbed' refers to the traditional practice of eating the chops directly by hand, without utensils, which is characteristic of Central Asian and Northwest Chinese food culture. The flavour profile is bold and smoky, with the warm earthiness of cumin dominating alongside a mild heat from chilli and a nutty finish from sesame. The texture contrasts a slightly crisp, spiced crust with succulent, well-marbled lamb meat. Nutritionally, hand-grabbed lamb chops are a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food providing approximately 235 kcal, 25.8 g protein, and 14.1 g fat per 100 g cooked weight. They are an excellent source of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and selenium. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable. Clinically, the absence of carbohydrate makes it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the saturated fat content warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Lanzhou beef noodle restaurants and Northwest Chinese specialty eateries, typically as a shared starter or main course priced at the premium end of the casual dining segment.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chinese lamb chops?
Are lamb chops good for diabetics?
What spices are used in Xinjiang lamb chops?
Is shou zhua yang pai gluten free?
Nutritional value of grilled lamb chops Chinese style

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Chinese-style hand-grabbed lamb chops exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy broiled lamb loin chop data, which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact preparation (seasoning blend, grilling method, bone-to-meat ratio) may vary by restaurant and the USDA source does not capture the specific Chinese spice profile.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 17283, broiled lamb loin chop) as no direct Singapore or Asian database entry exists for this specific preparation. 2. carbohydrate_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked lamb; the spice coating (cumin, chilli, sesame) contributes negligible carbohydrate at typical seasoning quantities. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for plain cooked lamb. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; lamb meat contains no dietary fibre. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lamb. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lamb loin chop. 7. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as meat contains no digestible carbohydrate and does not elicit a glycemic response; per Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is set to low (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 8. trans_fat_g of 0.5 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid present in ruminant fat, as reported by USDA; this is distinct from industrially produced trans fats. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g value of 5.9 g reported by USDA SR Legacy; sentinel -1 not used. 10. polyunsaturated_fat_g value of 1.1 g reported by USDA SR Legacy; sentinel -1 not used. 11. Halal status is flagged as true given the dish is lamb (permissible meat) and the restaurant context (Lanzhou beef noodle restaurant); individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 12. Sodium value of 72 mg per 100 g reflects the plain cooked lamb; restaurant preparations with added salt and seasoning will be higher. 13. No -1 sentinel values are present in this record; all nutrient fields are populated with values from USDA SR Legacy.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch using USDA SR Legacy as approved source. Populated all multilingual names for Chinese cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Documented all zero-value macros with confirmed-zero justification. Set GI to 0 with gi_category low and documented non-glycemic basis. Used closest_parent ontology match_type for all three ontology systems with gap notes.

Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 as required by Veritas v2 critical rule for effectively zero-GI foods. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and main notes to reflect this change and justify the value of 1. This resolved the q4_glycemic_completeness failure.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 aliases consistency failure. The Hindi alias in the flat aliases list contained a Latin character 'm' (typo: 'मेmने') instead of the correct Devanagari character 'म' ('मेमने'). Corrected the flat aliases entry to match the aliases_by_language Hindi entry exactly ('हाथ से पकड़े हुए मेमने के चॉप्स'). Performed full cross-check of all aliases_by_language strings against flat aliases list; all 15 entries now match verbatim.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
